title: Inside the Automats Where Coin-Operated Machines Created a Modern, Democratic Dining Experience
description: ãGood evening,ã said Alfred HitchôÙcock to the teleôÙviôÙsion viewôÙers of AmerôÙiôÙca on March 25, 1959. ãTonight Iãm dinôÙing at my favorite club.
body:
ãGood evening,ã said Alfred HitchôÙcock to the teleôÙviôÙsion viewôÙers of AmerôÙiôÙca on March 25, 1959. ãTonight Iãm dinôÙing at my favorite club. There are many advanôÙtages here. As you can see, inforôÙmalôÙiôÙty is the rule. There is also the stimôÙuôÙlaôÙtion of intelôÙlecôÙtuôÙal comôÙpanôÙionôÙship withôÙout the deafôÙenôÙing quiôÙet that perôÙvades most clubs. Best of all, I like its priôÙvaôÙcy: only four perôÙsons are allowed at a table, and, of course, no one pays any attenôÙtion to you.ã This was an examôÙple of the deadôÙpan irony with which the filmôÙmakôÙer introôÙduced each broadôÙcast of Alfred HitchôÙcock Presents, for the ãclubã of which he spoke was clearôÙly an automat. Today, many readôÙers under about 50 will nevôÙer have heard the word, butô at the time,ô it referred to a seemôÙingôÙly perôÙmaôÙnent instiôÙtuôÙtion in AmerôÙiôÙcan life.


	

Or rather, an instiôÙtuôÙtion of urban AmerôÙiôÙcan life, and above all in two cities, PhiladelôÙphia and New York. There, no one could think of automats withôÙout thinkôÙing of Horn & Hardart, in its heyôÙday the largest restauôÙrant chain in the world. The conôÙcept, which co-founder Joseph Horn importôÙed over from Berlin in the earôÙly nineôÙteen-tens, was of a restauôÙrant with no waitôÙers: rather, you could choose your dish û  la carte from a wall of coin-operôÙatôÙed comôÙpartôÙments, payôÙing the nickôÙel or two that would allow you to take the food inside.
SalôÙisôÙbury steak,ô creamed spinach, baked beans, a ham-and-cheese sandôÙwich, macôÙaôÙroni and cheese, chocoôÙlate pudôÙding, strawôÙberôÙry rhubarb pie: whatôÙevôÙer it was, the behind-the-scenes staff would replace it just as soon as you put the last one on your tray.


	

Smack of moderôÙniôÙty though it once did (and in a way, still does), the term automat is someôÙwhat misôÙleadôÙing. We might describe the expeôÙriôÙence of visôÙitôÙing one as dinôÙing inside a giant vendôÙing machine, but the actuôÙal runôÙning of the operôÙaôÙtion was quite labor-intenôÙsive. Most of the work was perôÙformed out of the cusôÙtomerãs sight, as far away as in the large cenôÙtral comôÙmisôÙsaries that preôÙpared many of the dishôÙes to be transôÙportôÙed daiôÙly to Horn & Hardartãs 88 locaôÙtions. This sheer scale of operôÙaôÙtion allowed the chain to offer some of the cheapôÙest meals comôÙmerôÙcialôÙly availôÙable, with the result that its automats boomed even ã indeed, espeôÙcialôÙly ã durôÙing the Great DepresôÙsion. Their ecoôÙnomôÙic barôÙriôÙer was low, and of sex and race, nonexôÙisôÙtent; those who rememôÙber them describe them becomôÙing some of the most demoôÙcôÙraôÙtôÙic instiôÙtuôÙtions in postôÙwar AmerôÙiôÙca.


	

You can hear such memôÙoôÙries recalled inô the recent docôÙuôÙmenôÙtaryô The Automatô by figôÙures like Ruth BadôÙer GinsôÙburg, ColôÙin PowôÙell, and Mel Brooks, who rhapôÙsodizes about Horn & Hardartãs cofôÙfee,ô disôÙpensed for just a nickôÙel from elabôÙoôÙrate dolôÙphin-headôÙed spigôÙots. That degree of detail was stanôÙdard in the inteôÙriôÙors, whose marôÙble, chrome, and glass look palaôÙtial by the stanôÙdards of the fast-food joints that ultiôÙmateôÙly replaced the automat. That gloôÙry was one casuôÙalôÙty of postôÙwar subôÙurôÙbanôÙizaôÙtion and holôÙlowôÙing-out of cenôÙtral cities that resultôÙed. What with the AmerôÙiôÙcan urban renaisôÙsance of the past few decades, attempts have been made to revive the automat conôÙcept, but perôÙhaps, as Brooks puts it, ãthe logisôÙtics and the ecoôÙnomôÙics of today wonãt allow anyôÙthing that simôÙple, naû₤ve, and eloôÙquent and beauôÙtiôÙful to flourôÙish again.ã OrderôÙing a meal brought straight to your door may be more conôÙveôÙnient, but even delivôÙery-app addicts have to admit that it will nevôÙer have the same romance.
RelatôÙed conôÙtent:
Slot Machine Age: A 1964 British NewsôÙreel Angsts Over Whether AutoôÙmatôÙed Machines Will DisôÙplace PeoôÙple
How Edward Hopperãs PaintôÙings Inspired the Creepy SusôÙpense of Alfred Hitchcockãs Rear WinôÙdow
Watch the ãBibôÙlio-Matã Book-VendôÙing Machine DisôÙpense LitôÙerôÙary Delight
Behold the Art-o-Mat: VinôÙtage CigôÙaôÙrette VendôÙing Machines Get RepurôÙposed & DisôÙpense Works of Art
How Fast Food Began: The HisôÙtoôÙry of This ThorôÙoughôÙly AmerôÙiôÙcan (and Now GlobôÙal) Form of DinôÙing
Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: AI Figures Out the Rules of a Mysterious 2,000-Year-Old Board Game from Ancient Rome
description:
in Games, History |   	March 9th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			
Image by WalôÙter Crist
As far as enthuôÙsiôÙasm for board games goes, no conôÙtiôÙnent has
body:
in Games, History |   	March 9th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			
Image by WalôÙter Crist
As far as enthuôÙsiôÙasm for board games goes, no conôÙtiôÙnent has yet outôÙdone Europe. Its advanôÙtage could lie in the highôÙly develôÙoped culôÙture of low-cost leisure eviôÙdent in quite a few of its sociôÙeties; it could also owe to the fact that board games seem to have been played there conôÙtinôÙuôÙousôÙly since antiqôÙuiôÙty. Weãve long had eviôÙdence of examôÙples like the ãRoman mill game,ã betôÙter known today as nine menãs morôÙris, which Ovid appears to menôÙtion in his Ars AmaôÙtoôÙriaô of the very earôÙly first cenôÙtuôÙry. Not that modôÙern knowlôÙedge of Roman tableôÙtop gamôÙing is comôÙplete. In one puzôÙzling case, the stone board above was unearthed in a forôÙmer Roman town in the NetherôÙlands, but how a game was played on it remained a mysôÙtery ã until machine learnôÙing came along.
ãTo examôÙine whether the object may have been used as a game board, we perôÙformed use-wear analyôÙsis to idenôÙtiôÙfy eviôÙdence for gameôÙplay and we simôÙuôÙlatôÙed play using artiôÙfiôÙcial intelôÙliôÙgence (AI),ã write the team of researchers who recentôÙly pubôÙlished a paper on the subôÙject inô the jourôÙnalô AntiqôÙuiôÙty. They used a sysôÙtem called Ludii, engiôÙneered to anaôÙlyze board-game rules.
ãThis softôÙware allows for AI-driôÙven playôÙout simôÙuôÙlaôÙtion, where two AI agents play a game against one anothôÙer, which can genôÙerôÙate quanôÙtiôÙtaôÙtive data on gameôÙplay. In this instance, we explored whether the rules of a game would proôÙduce the wear patôÙtern seen on the stone.ã The idea, in othôÙer words, was to let the comôÙputôÙer play against itself using difôÙferôÙent rules until it came upon a game that would conôÙtinôÙue to abrade away the surôÙface of the board in the same fashôÙion as it already was.
This process narôÙrowed it down to games ãin which the goal is to block the oppoôÙnent from movôÙing, and those in which the goal is to place three pieces in a row.ã These have a fairôÙly long docôÙuôÙmentôÙed hisôÙtoôÙry, from ScanôÙdiôÙnaviôÙaãs hareôÙtavl, to Italyãs gioôÙco dellãorsoô to Spainãs liebre perseguiôÙda, to Greeceãs kinûˋôÙgi tou lagoû¤. You can downôÙload what the research sugôÙgests is the most plauôÙsiôÙble rule set for this parôÙticôÙuôÙlar Roman board game here, board design includôÙed. One playôÙer takes the side of the ãhunter,ã with four pieces, and the othôÙer the side of the ãprey,ã with two. The forôÙmer tries to trap the latôÙterãs pieces, movôÙing only along the boardôÙãs lines; in the next round, the roles reverse. The hunter who does the job in the fewest moves wins. Why not invite friends over to spend an evening playôÙing like a Roman? For a thorôÙoughôÙly ancient good time, first reconôÙstruct as best you can the ambiôÙence of the therôÙmopoliôÙumô at home.
RelatôÙed conôÙtent:
Watch a Playthrough of the OldôÙest Board Game in the World, the SumerôÙian RoyôÙal Game of Ur, CirôÙca 2500 BC
Behold the First AmerôÙiôÙcan Board Game, TravôÙellersã Tour Through the UnitôÙed States (1822)
MonopôÙoly: How the OrigôÙiôÙnal Game Was Made to ConôÙdemn MonopôÙoôÙlies & the AbusôÙes of CapôÙiôÙtalôÙism
Kurt Vonnegutãs Lost Board Game Is FinalôÙly for Sale
The FiendishôÙly ComôÙpliôÙcatôÙed Board Game That Takes 1,500 Hours to Play: DisôÙcovôÙer The CamôÙpaign for North Africa
The Fear and Loathing in Las Vegas Board Game, Inspired by Hunter S. Thompsonãs RolôÙlickôÙing NovôÙel
Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: Elizabeth Saloka's Vibrant Painted Rocks Adopt the Personalities of Snacks and Pop Culture Icons
description: While most of us will pass by stray stones and piles of rubble without much of a second thought, Elizabeth Saloka sees tons of potential. From a c
body:
While most of us will pass by stray stones and piles of rubble without much of a second thought, Elizabeth Saloka sees tons of potential. From a couple of rock piles outside of her regular supermarket to crumbling curbs or demolished structures, she sifts through a variety of shapes and sizes to find rocks that may eventually transform into vibrant mimics of common household items, boxed sandwiches from Pret a Manger, or Babybel brand snacking cheese.
ãLast fall, I bought a ton of marble scraps off a sculptor in Woodstock for like, $10 off Facebook,ã Saloka tells Colossal. ãFor sandwiches and cakes, crumbling asphalt parking lots are good. When I lived in Sunset Park, they demolished a building a couple blocks from my apartment, and there was a hole in the fence, so Iãd go in there and find tons of cool shapes and textures of rubble.ã

Using bricks, she creates humorously fat stacks of $1 and $5 bills, and cut pavers become Premium saltine cracker boxes. ãThat particular rock shapeãa long rectangular cubeãis to me the holy grail of rock shapes, because it doesnãt really naturally occur too much in nature,ã Saloka says. When she finds a particular shape or cut that works well for certain objects, such as Pink Pearl erasers or popular candies, she collects as many as she can.
Pop culture items rule Salokaãs oeuvre, from recognizable grocery store brands to fast food mainstays to memes like the so-called ãIKEA monkeyã in its tiny shearling coat. Thereãs also an air of nostalgia for toys, analog film, and pantry staples that many of us associate with our childhoods or an earlier generation.
If youãre in New York, you can see the artistãs work in Snacks and the City at Gothamãs Chelsea gallery space, which runs from March 19 to May 3. Her work will also be including a group show at Galactic Panther, which will be on view from March 27 to May 21. And in June, sheãll participate in a two-person show alongside Kate Bingaman-Burt at Womenãs Studio Workshop and a solo exhibition at Here to Sunday. See more on Salokaãs Instagram.

schema.headline: Elizabeth Saloka's Vibrant Painted Rocks Adopt the Personalities of Snacks and Pop Culture Icons

title: In Paraguay, Architecture Doesn't Come at the Expense of Nature at 'Un Bosque en La Casa'
description: A new home designed by Equipo de Arquitectura begs the question: is it a house in a forest or a forest in a house? The name of the project sheds s
body:
A new home designed by Equipo de Arquitectura begs the question: is it a house in a forest or a forest in a house? The name of the project sheds some light on that, aptly titled ãUn Bosque en la Casa,ã or ãA Forest in the House.ã Bricks, steel, glass, and concrete combine in a single-story contemporary home thatãs all corners, volume, and apertures, while the trees and tropical plants around it organically soften its angles.
Architects Horacio Cherniavsky and Viviana Pozzoli took the lead on this new home in San Bernardino, Paraguay, challenging the notion that nature is in direct opposition to development. ããA Forest in the Houseã proposes an alternative approach to harmonizing the built form with its natural surroundings,ã the studio says. ãRather than treating existing trees as obstacles, the project embraces them as fundamental guides that shape the spatial program.ã See more on the firmãs Instagram.

schema.headline: In Paraguay, Architecture Doesn't Come at the Expense of Nature at 'Un Bosque en La Casa'

title: Amoako Boafo Takes His Studio on the Road
description:
Art Review
                His new exhibition "I Bring Home With Me" combines portraits with seating areas and a model of his st
body:
Art Review
                His new exhibition "I Bring Home With Me" combines portraits with seating areas and a model of his studio, inviting visitors to stay awhile and get comfortable.
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
                
        
            Installation view of Amoako Boafo: I Bring Home With Me at Roberts Projects, Los Angeles (courtesy the artist and Roberts Projects, Los Angeles; photo Paul Salveson)
    
        
            LOS ANGELES ã In the heart of Los Angeles,ô Amoako Boafo's I Bring Home With Me at Roberts Projects offers a respite from the chaotic energy of nearby Hollywood and all its thirsty worshippers. The new exhibition of lauded Vienna-based painter Amoako Boafo is a portal to a different city, in a vastly different part of the world: his hometown of Accra, Ghana. Through 22 portraits, including two of himself, done in his signature elegantly rustic style of collaged finger trails instead of typical brushstrokes, the artist introduces viewers to the people, faces, and patterns shaping his sense of place. ô This exhibition features some firsts for Boafoãs US audience. It is the first time weãve seen him incorporate embroidery into his portraits, adding dimension to his immortalized subjects. More notably, it is his first stateside partnership with Glenn DeRoche of DeRoche Projects, an Accra-based architecture and design firm. DeRoche produced a to-scale recreation of Boafoãs Accra studio within the Roberts Projects exhibition space. The intention was to utilize exposed Ghanaian teakwood, but turbulent international tariffs waylaid that plan. Instead, the structure is constructed of local plywood painted black. I visited the show twice ã once for the press preview and again 12 days later. This second time, I was struck by how the atmosphere had transformed. Given time for the installation to breathe, a wooden, earthen musk permeated the gallery. Though Iãm sure itãs not the scent of Ghana, it helped me to leave Los Angeles behind, to slow my breathing and receive the work differently.ô Installation view of Amoako Boafo: I Bring Home With Me at Roberts Projects, Los Angeles (courtesy the artist and Roberts Projects, Los Angeles; photo Paul Salveson)DeRocheãs structure mimics Boafoãs studio but also evokes a communal gathering space, with a family table area scattered with playing cards displaying Boafoãs work and a seating area recalling a living room ã its furniture covered in fabric printed with Boafoãs previous works. ãA Living Space,ã a poem by Ghanaian-born poet Yielu, plays on a loop. Visitors can flip through books on Boafoãs artistic contemporaries and inspirations from home and abroad. It all suggests that one stay awhile, get comfortable.ô Boafoãs techniques bring a physicality to his paintings that mirrors the tactility of the space. When working on his subjectsã faces and flesh, he paints with his fingers, the visceral texture deepening the humanity of each portrait. Much as we are formed by and within bodies, these likenesses by Boafo come to life in a similar manner. Blues, blacks, browns, and grays coalesce through the artistãs fingertip to emulate the kaleidoscope effect deep skin tones can often hold. His hand rendering of their features makes the showãs title that much more apt and profound.ô Installation view of portraits in Amoako Boafo: I Bring Home With Me at Roberts Projects, Los Angeles (photo Nereya Otieno/Hyperallergic)Dominating the show are regal portraits of femme subjects. From their frames flows a feminine resiliency and commitment to duty, place, people that enables simple rooms to become safe spaces, for groups to be transformed into community. I feel that Boafo is honoring that femme entity in homemaking ã in the portraits of himself along with those of the others. Each work projects the willful act of welcoming to create a sense of home ã particularly ãFloral Shirt,ã which looked over me while I eased into the living room, her face framed by voluminous hair, her swirled-finger-trail-skin coming across as 3D as she kept me company on the couch. Shifting my gaze, I found a wall of seven other women doing the same: meeting my eyes and acknowledging my presence in their world.ô This observation of homemaking is also code for those who are part of a diaspora. Whether they have traveled by necessity or choice, the recreation of home is a part of each personãs tale. Here, Boafo crosses land and sea and puts color to canvas to lay bare his origins for us. He invites you to sit down in his home and greet the people there as they look back at you.Installation view of Amoako Boafo: I Bring Home With Me at Roberts Projects, Los Angeles (photo Nereya Otieno/Hyperallergic)Installation view of Amoako Boafo, "Lemon Slip Blouse" (2025) in I Bring Home With Me at Roberts Projects, Los Angeles (photo Nereya Otieno/Hyperallergic)Installation view of Amoako Boafo, "Self Portrait ã Ivy Leaf Sofa" (2025) in I Bring Home With Me at Roberts Projects, Los Angeles (photo Nereya Otieno/Hyperallergic)Amoako Boafo: I Bring Home With Me continues at Roberts Projects (442 South La Brea Avenue, Hancock Park, Los Angeles) through March 21.ô The exhibition was organized by the gallery.
                  
                      
                        Art Review
                      
                      
                        Review
                      
                      
                        Amoako Boafo
                      
                      
                        Roberts Projects
                      
                      
                        Los Angeles

title: Thaddeus Mosley, Beloved Self-Taught Sculptor, Dies at 99
description: Thaddeus Mosley, a self-taught artist who became an internationally known sculptor and a beloved Pittsburgh public figure, died on Friday
body:
Thaddeus Mosley, a self-taught artist who became an internationally known sculptor and a beloved Pittsburgh public figure, died on Friday, March 6, at the age of 99.Members of the artistãs family, including Pittsburgh City Councilor Khari Mosley, one of his six children, announced that he had died at his home after spending time in hospice care.In a statement he shared on social media, Khari Mosley described his father as a ãdedicated family man, ubiquitous community pillar, and an inimitable creative force who embodied the hard-working ethos of his blue-collar Western Pennsylvanian roots and the innovative essence of the classic jazz music that served as his spiritual inspiration.ãFor more than 70 years, Mosley carved figures using mallets and chisels out of salvaged wood from Pennsylvania forests before finding acclaim in his ninth decade with shows at the Musûˋe National Eugû´ne Delacroix in Paris, Bergen Kunsthall in Norway, the Baltimore Museum of Art, the Nasher Sculpture Center in Dallas, and elsewhere.Thaddeus Mosley in 1957. (Courtesy Karma and the estate of Thaddeus Mosley) Mosley frequently characterized his method as ãsculptural improvisation.ã Inspired by Constantin Brancusi, African tribal art, and Isamu Noguchi, for whom he had a particular affinity, as well as classical jazz, he approached his work by letting the forms of his raw materials speak to him. Instead of making variations on a theme or imposing a shape onto logs of wood, Mosley used a chisel and gauge to respond to the natural gradations of its surface.ô ãI try to make things that generate their own spirituality so that people might feel something about it,ã Mosley wrote in a 2021 exhibition catalogue of his work at Karma Gallery. ãWhat presence is to make something have a life of its own ã the alchemy of turning something natural into something alive.ãMosley was born in 1926 in New Castle, Pennsylvania, a small city about 50 miles northwest of Pittsburgh, as one of five children to a coal miner. He served in the US Navy during World War II and graduated from the University of Pittsburgh in 1950 with a degree in English.He stayed in the steel city to become a sportswriter for the Pittsburgh Courier, a weekly African-American newspaper. Then he worked other jobs, including a four-decade career as a mail sorter for the United States Postal Service, while beginning to carve two-by-four planks of wood into animal figures that were displayed at a Downtown Pittsburgh department store.A close-up encounter with Noguchiãs work in 1959 proved significant. A friend photographing the sets of a Pittsburgh performance of Martha Grahamãs ballet Appalachian Spring invited Mosely to carry some equipment in order to get a better look at Noguchiãs set design for the show. Mosley began making more abstract pieces in the vein of Noguchi and other sculptors like Brancusi and Alberto Giacometti.ô ãMy main idea, of course, is the idea of weight in space, and that idea is that the piece, the sculpture, should look like it's levitating, it should look like there is movement,ã Mosley said in an interview with the Seattle Art Museum.A decade later, Mosley met Noguchi at a lecture the Japanese American artist gave at the Carnegie Institute, now the Carnegie Museum of Art. Noguchi was already aware of his work; then-director Leon Arkus championed Mosleyãs art by exhibiting a solo show and displaying one of his pieces on the museum's stairs.ô He remained virtually unknown outside of Pittsburgh, despite several group and solo shows at the Carnegie Museum and a number of large public sculptures commissioned throughout the city. That began to change in 2018 when his work was included in Carnegie International, the 57th edition of the international art show, at the age of 92.ô Soon, commercial galleries started calling and Mosley went on to display sculptures in New York, London, Amsterdam, Norway, Western Massachusetts, and Maine. His exhibition at Karma Gallery in 2020, just before the pandemic hit, paid tribute to his artistic heroes while also referencing his longtime love of jazz. ãI never know exactly what Iãm doing. Thatãs also the essence of good jazz,ã a press release for the exhibition said.ô In a review of the show, Hyperallergicãs John Yau wrote that he appreciated how Mosley inserted a ãsharp knowing witã into his freestanding vertical pieces. He argued the art world should have been paying attention to Mosleyãs genius decades ago.ãAs reasons for this belated awareness, we might consider the rise of the investment class of collectors; racism in the guise of supposedly neutral theory; and the continuing triumph of period styles and theoretical positions over artists who determinedly follow their own trajectory,ã wrote Yau.
                  
                      
                        News
                      
                      
                        Obituaries
                      
                      
                        Thaddeus Mosley

title: Chronicles of a Needless War
description:
Daily Newsletter
                DCãs ãJeffrey Epstein Walk of Shame,ã a major ruling on copyrights for AI art, Israel-US strike
body:
Daily Newsletter
                DCãs ãJeffrey Epstein Walk of Shame,ã a major ruling on copyrights for AI art, Israel-US strikes damage a historic site in Tehran, exhibitions to visit in Los Angeles this month, and more.
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
        
            As Lunar New Year celebrations continue across the world, nobody embodies the holiday spirit quite like New Yorkers. Case in point: AX Mina visited Abrons Arts Center for its annual mutual aid initiative and art exhibition. From Chinatown, with Love celebrates the kaleidoscope of queer, working-class imagery and art that makes the community so special ã and amid a world in turmoil, Mina's reflections remind us of what the Year of the Fire Horse truly represents.Meanwhile, Israel and US airstrikes on Tehran damage priceless artifacts in the Qajar-era Golestan Palace as a ãJeffrey Epstein Walk of Shameã guerrilla installation in DC reminds us of the real reason why Trump waged this needless war.ãLakshmi Rivera Amin, associate editor
Installation view of From Chinatown, With Love (photo AX Mina/Hyperallergic)The Political Potential of the Chinatown StorefrontAbrons Arts Center is hosting its annual Lunar New Year mutual aid initiative, where art highlights and supports local businesses. | AX Mina
                    
                    
                    
                        
                            Artists Thinking Out Loud: The IFPDA Returns to the Park Avenue Armory this AprilThe fair will bring together 80 exhibitors and an expanded focus on drawings in a nod to the mediumãs long-standing relationship with printmaking.
                        
                        
                            Learn more
                        
                    
            NewsGolestan Palace on March 3 after it was damaged during US and Israeli airstrikes in Tehran, Iran (photo by Majid Saeedi via Getty Images)Israeli and American airstrikes on Iran haveô caused significant damageô to the Qajar-eraartifacts, aô UNESCO World Heritage Siteô in the heart of Tehran.In Washington, DC, a "Jeffrey Epstein Walk of Shame" includes ãstarsã for MoMA trustee Leon Black, arts patron Les Wexner, and others mentioned in the Epstein files.Computer scientistô Stephen Thalerô struck out once again this week after the US Supreme Court declined to hear his appeal on the years-long legal battle to secure copyright protections for an AI-generated ãartwork.ãFrom Our CriticsInstallation view of Petrit Halilaj, ãShkrepeätima (Nita)ã (2018) (photo by Cat Dawson/Hyperallergic)Petrit Halilajãs Opera of Kosovan Memory and MythThrough his fantastical vignettes, Halilaj suggests curiosity about others as a way to neutralize the forces that lead to difference-based violence. | Cat Dawson
                    
                    
                    
                        
                            Prisoners of Love: Until the Sun of FreedomThe only US presentation of this exhibition by renowned artists Basel Abbas and Ruanne Abou-Rahme is on view at The Bell Gallery, Brown University.
